title: 2025/09/10 - GA Subcommittee A (2of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-09-10 - Governance and Administration Committee - Online Casino Gambling Bill [Subcommittee A]
description: Wednesday, 10th September 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Online Casino Gambling Bill Hearings: Hawkes Bay Dragon Boat Club Leonie Wallwork, Chair Michelle Danks, Treasurer and Fundraising Chair Nancy Scott, Club Captain Papatoetoe Wildcats American Football Club Victoria Theatre Trust Canterbury Alpine Ice Skating Club Logan Fort, Funding Administrator Lake House Arts Grae, Manager 888NZ Lonnie Bossi, Chief Executive Officer Itai Pazner Yaniv Sherman Sasha Grist
descriptionSummary: Online Casino Gambling Bill. Hearings. Hawkes Bay Dragon Boat Club

chunks.text: Fantastic. Um, welcome. It's great to see you all there. Thank you for, um, joining us today. So for us to do that, um, we're absolutely reliant on, uh, community funding and especially the gambling trust funding, um, you know, to support what we do. But also because we are the only club in Hawkes Bay, we had to travel out of the region to compete, to participate, and to qualify.

Um, and so to do that, obviously there's a cost, and on average it's about eight to $10,000 every time we attend over gata. That is, has been, uh, funded through the gambling trusts in the, you know, up to date and hopefully into the future.

Um, we also have a challenge here with, um, I guess local government with the sinking LID policy. So we're getting squeezed in both directions around, um, the gambling side of things, um, and the closing down or the sinking lid on, uh, venues that are offering that.

Um, it's very contestable. So we, um, it's very challenging. We apply for our funding, um, ahead of our events. Uh, and often we, we do find already that we can be unsuccessful because of Lacker funds. Um, and this is only gonna further compound and put the, uh, the squeeze on us, but that if we didn't get funding for an event, um, it would cost our members five to $600 each to attend an event, and that's just not possible at all.

Um, so just a little bit about Dragon boating. It, it's more than a sport, it's a lifeline in so many ways. Um, so it, um, it's for all ages above 12, and we have members, um, quite a few members, quite a few of us actually in that older age bracket, but it's great for social, uh, connection, uh, physical fitness, mental health.

Um, we also have a breast cancer survivor crew, and there are a number of those crews throughout New Zealand. Um, so it's an absolutely vital, and it's proven to benefit the health benefits of that crew. So for us to train and compete as a breast cancer team around the country, we need the funding to support us to do that.

Um, it's proven to be a powerful tool for mental health, physical rehabilitation and social inclusion, as I said. And we are, as I said, in a growth phase. We've only been operating five to six years, um, and looking to buy more boats, more equipment, and all of that's a cost of boats, $15,000, you know, our life jackets, uh, paddles, all of that equipment for water safety.

We do water safety training. Um, we have training of our crews. So we offer a number of, um, benefits to our community, not just, um, us as a team or as a crew or as a club, um, with open days, um, and engaging with our community. There's only so much that a sausage sizzle or a quiz night will go towards.

Um, and that usually covers some of our overheads, like insurance and container high for our equipment and things like that, but it goes nowhere to getting us equipment, um, or getting us to the competitions if we missed on, you know, if this, uh, went ahead and there was no mandated minimum of a 40% return back to community, um, for us that would price our participation out of the market.

I just, there is no other, you know, avenue, um, for us to do that. Sorry. Um, so our ability to compete, to grow, to engage with our community would be, uh, severely disadvantaged and you can't grow, um, a sport or a team or a, um, a community service on barren land.

So, uh, it, it, it's a vital lifeline for us. Um, and I also think that, you know, it's people at gamble in our local venues. We, uh, support those local venues with our, um, our meetings, our quiz nights. So it's a two way, uh, support mechanism within our community.

Um, we already feel the impact of the reduced class four funding with the policies. As I said, especially one of our local governments is, uh, clamping down on that quite hard. Um, and also with, uh, you know, some online gambling that was sort of came on the back end of, uh, COVID and things like that.

So that ripple effect is still being felt. Um, there is, with the increased contestability, there's clubs like ours and many other sporting clubs or, um, community, um, organizations that are, are missing out. It's, it's crazy to put us all in, fighting against each other for, um, every little dollar that we can to survive.

Um, so, um, we've raised, um, our own membership fees. We charge our members, um, a fee for their uniforms. Um, yeah, so the bottom line is we would not be able to travel. We would not be able to compete, and as I said, probably over 80% of our club members would not be able to, um, self-fund this, the participation in the sport. Um, so essentially we're on this growth trajectory. It's very exciting. We've got, um, a very committed crew.

We have, um, a coach that travels through from Wellington at his own expense every week, uh, to train us. So he really believes in us and what we are doing. Um, but all of that would diminish without us being able to access the gambling trust funding. Um, so I guess in looking at the broader picture, which I'm sure this message is coming through loud and clear with the committee anyway, um, but we'll just add our voice to it anyway.

Um, class four gambling returns approximately 300 million annually to over 11,000 community groups. Um, and, you know, and sporting groups and, um, the online casinos currently return less than 2% of profits to communities if the bill proceeds without safeguards.

There are millions of dollars we strip from our regional communities. Um, and you know, I I just think the regional regions are struggling anyway in so many ways. Um, and um, this would just be another, uh, death nail to that. Um, so yeah, we are just asking that we're not asking for charity, but we are asking for fairness.

And that, um, a recognition that sports like dragon boating saves lives, builds resilience and connects communities. And without the funding, we cannot grow. We cannot compete and we cannot serve our communities. The government must regulate responsibly and ensure gambling profits continue to strengthen the regions who need them most.

Um, so Koto and thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Sise Henry, and I am here on behalf of the Papa Toi Toi Wildcats American Football Club in Auckland, um, as well as our peers across the American football community.

Collectively, there are 21 American football clubs nationwide, and we are here regarding the OCG, the online casino gambling Bill and I, I wanna be clear from the outset that we asked the committee to include a community benefit clause in this bill to ensure gambling profits are reinvested back into New Zealand communities so we can all be happy and healthy.

So the Pepper Toi Wildcats, we are proud to be celebrating our 39th year across our community and from local clubs right through to our national body. Actually, we're heavily reliant on community funding to survive for us and many other sports in our tero community.

Funding, um, is the difference between a healthy New Zealander and an unhealthy one, a good citizen and a disengaged one. And in some cases it could be the difference between life and death. Now, community grant funding and gaming trust funding, we use this funding to reduce the cost of participation in our sport.

Without it, many of our young people would simply just not be able to play American football. If you're familiar, it's like the NFL. We have shoulders and pads. Um, so we demand specialized, important, imported equipment to safely field one athlete. And this could cost us from 800 to a thousand New Zealand dollars per athlete.

And they are only manufactured in the USA now for a lot of our families already under financial pressure, that cost is impossible without, uh, the support of community funding. So it makes it possible for them to pass a participate. Amir American Football is the sport.

It is unique because we are the most inclusive sport in the world. There is a role for every body type with a big or small, tall or short, and it's a true team sport. So success requires every single player on the grid iron to do their role, and nobody is left out.

It is also a pathway sport so our athletes can access higher education through the, through US scholarships, creating life-changing opportunities, not just for individuals, but for families and generations to come. Just this year, our club alone had 11 athletes represent New Zealand, um, raising their profile for international opportunities and lifting the pride of their AU and committees, especially in a time where it's been winter, it's been cold, there's not too much happening in school.

There's been something that a family can look forward to. Um, so Papa Toy Toy, if you're not familiar, we are located in South Auckland. Um, we are an area of hyper deprivation, low income, high obesity rates, and high levels of suicide and depression. What our club and other clubs like it do is we provide a safe space where people can thrive.

Um, for example, one young man joined us and he was weighing over a hundred kgs and battling depression. With the support of his teammates, he lost 30 kgs. He's gained a whole bunch of confidence. He's now, um, employed and he's mentoring others.

We've seen women overcome postnatal depression, and we've seen young people escape bullying by finding belonging in a team. Uh, these short stories, they're not isolated stories. They're a daily reality of what our what community sport achieves when supported. Um, American football, we may not be the largest sport, but we are one of the most diverse and inclusive.

So 4,500 odd play across New Zealand of this about 50%, uh, Maori and Pacifica. So, uh, specifically 2,144 Pacifica 700 to 64 Maori. And our youngest players, just like the dragging boating girls are 10 to 12 and they can play until they decide they don't want to play anymore.

We do have intergenerational participation with granddad's sons, sons, or sons and daughters. Um, this inclusivity is essential to who we are, but it also means we represent communities who are disproportionately affected by gambling harm, which makes the return of gambling profits, her community funding not just desirable, but necessary.

Um, the OCG bill we agree is an important step, but, and this it includes a community benefit clause. Gambling profits will flow offshore and into private hands and not return to the communities who bear the greatest social costs. Um, a community benefit clause would guarantee profits are reinvested locally supporting grassroots sports, social services, and community organizations.

Again, healthy, happy New Zealanders, uh, maintain fairness, ensuring that very, ensuring the very communities most impacted by gambling harm also see the greatest benefit from returns. And it protects sports likes where survival depends on contestable funding. Without the safeguard, our communities kind of realistically we lose twice, once through the harm caused by gambling and then again when the profits are taken away from local reinvestment.

Um, and then in closing, community funding, it's not optional for us, it's essential. It keeps our young people active, safe included, and connected. It creates pathways to higher education and it's an opportunity to save lives. On behalf of the Papa to Wildcats and our peer clubs, the 21 other American football clubs across, we strongly urge you to please include a community benefit clause in the online casino gambling bill, because when gambling profits fail to return to the communities who bear the social cost, the harm is doubled. Thank you.

In my submission, I would like to make a case for amendment of the online casino gambling bill in consideration of the regulated return of online gambling profits to communities. Yearly. Machine profits to communities through contestable funding. They generate pride, unity, and wellbeing. They are the net positive that justifies gambling's social license. The support from that funding for arts centers like Lakehouse Arts and our North Shore partners ranges from 2.5% up to 25% or more of overall revenue per annum.

Income helps fund individual projects, subsidized community activities, essential facilities, upkeep and maintenance. And Lakehouse is just one example across roa, anywhere up to $300 million a year in gaming. Machine returns, sustained sports clubs, marae committees, cultural festivals, and community centers.

If online gambling takes player spend away without giving back, we will see machines removed. As venues become unsustainable. A permanent decline in community grants and thousands of programs, festivals, and scholarships across the country cut back or entirely lost.

The long-term outcome is diminished society, fewer places where people gather, learn, and celebrate together. If that funding support from gaming machines diminishes. The impacts for us are immediate. Our scholarships employ professional art teachers who mentor children over years.

Without continued funding support, these scholarships disappear and so do teaching hours. Artists lose income and children lose opportunity. Every free event we run requires staff coordinators and also maintenance and upkeep. A reduction in grants means fewer shifts, less employment and scaling back of events.

Keeping our facilities well kept in The safest, most accessible condition would be under threat, driving up costs of services to support maintenance and reducing access to those who need it the most. And most importantly, families who cannot afford to pay will be shut out of opportunities that enrich their lives.

Leaving the arts as a privilege for the few not a right for all members of parliament, this bill will be your legacy if passed in its current form at risks being remembered as the legislation that cut off the lifeblood of grassroots culture, sport and community across New Zealand for generations to come, people will look back and ask, why was the principle of community return abandoned when gambling moved on online?

However, this is also an opportunity to support community wellbeing and legislative bill that supports more and better social license and benefit that supports community wellbeing and provides avenues for community prosperity. In closing, I urge you to amend this bill in consideration of regulated return of online gambling profits two communities.
